class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Financial Accounting and the Accounting Equation
learning_objectives: Explain the purpose and users of financial accounting information
State and apply the accounting equation: Assets = Liabilities + Equity
Identify the four primary financial statements

lecture_notes.introduction: Financial accounting is the language of business. This course teaches you to read, prepare, and interpret financial statements that companies use to communicate their financial health.
key_concepts: The accounting equation: A = L + E
Financial statements: income statement, balance sheet, cash flow, equity
GAAP and the role of FASB
Users of financial information: investors, creditors, regulators
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk-through of Apple's most recent 10-K filing, identifying each financial statement
setup_requirements: SEC EDGAR access for company filings

review.value: Assets = Liabilities + Stockholders' Equity; every transaction must keep this equation in balance
Revenue recognized when earned, expenses when incurred, regardless of cash timing

learning_objectives: Analyze business transactions using debit and credit rules
Record journal entries for common transactions

lecture_notes.introduction: Double-entry bookkeeping is the foundation of all accounting systems. Every transaction has two sides, ensuring the books always balance.
key_concepts: Debit and credit rules for each account type
Normal balances: assets/expenses are debits, liabilities/equity/revenue are credits
lecture_notes.demonstration: Recording a full month of transactions for a new consulting firm, from journal entries through trial balance

review.value: Assets and expenses increase with debits; liabilities, equity, and revenues increase with credits
A list of all accounts and their balances to verify total debits equal total credits

class_schedule.topic: Adjusting Entries and the Accounting Cycle
learning_objectives: Explain why adjusting entries are necessary
Prepare adjusting entries for deferrals and accruals

lecture_notes.introduction: At the end of each period, adjusting entries ensure that revenues and expenses are recorded in the correct period, following the matching principle.
key_concepts: Closing entries and post-closing trial balance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Complete accounting cycle exercise from unadjusted trial balance through financial statements

review.value: Cash received/paid before revenue earned/expense incurred; recognized over time
Revenue earned/expense incurred before cash received/paid; recorded via adjusting entry

class_schedule.topic: The Income Statement and Revenue Recognition
learning_objectives: Apply the five-step revenue recognition model (ASC 606)
Calculate and interpret gross profit margin and operating margin

lecture_notes.introduction: The income statement reports a company's financial performance over a period. Understanding revenue recognition is critical for accurate reporting.
key_concepts: Cost of goods sold and gross profit
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a multi-step income statement from raw transaction data and calculating key profitability ratios

review.value: Identify contract, identify obligations, determine price, allocate price, recognize when obligations satisfied
(Revenue - COGS) / Revenue; measures profitability of core operations before operating expenses

class_schedule.topic: The Balance Sheet and Financial Position
learning_objectives: Classify assets, liabilities, and equity on a balance sheet
Calculate current ratio and debt-to-equity ratio
Interpret balance sheet data for decision making

lecture_notes.introduction: The balance sheet provides a snapshot of a company's financial position at a specific point in time, showing what it owns, owes, and the residual value for shareholders.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative balance sheet analysis of two competing companies in the same industry

review.value: Current Assets / Current Liabilities; measures short-term liquidity (healthy is typically > 1.5)
Total Liabilities / Total Equity; measures financial leverage and long-term solvency

learning_objectives: Classify cash flows into operating, investing, and financing activities
Prepare a cash flow statement using the indirect method
Analyze free cash flow and its significance

lecture_notes.introduction: The statement of cash flows reveals how a company generates and uses cash, bridging the gap between accrual-based income and actual cash position.
key_concepts: Investing activities: asset purchases and sales
Financing activities: debt and equity transactions
Free cash flow = Operating CF - Capital expenditures
Cash flow vs. net income discrepancies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Preparing a complete cash flow statement from comparative balance sheets and income statement data

review.value: Start with net income, adjust for non-cash items (depreciation) and changes in working capital accounts
Cash available after maintaining/expanding asset base; key indicator of financial flexibility

questions.question: A company purchases $15,000 of equipment by paying $5,000 cash and signing a $10,000 note payable. Show how this transaction affects the accounting equation and prepare the journal entry.
questions.answer: Accounting equation: Assets increase by $15,000 (Equipment +$15,000, Cash -$5,000, net +$10,000) and Liabilities increase by $10,000. Journal entry: Debit Equipment $15,000; Credit Cash $5,000; Credit Notes Payable $10,000.

rubric.key: Correct journal entry with debits and credits

questions.question: Explain why accrual basis accounting provides a more accurate picture of a company's financial performance than cash basis accounting.
questions.answer: Accrual basis records revenues when earned and expenses when incurred, matching them to the period in which economic activity occurs. Cash basis only records transactions when cash changes hands, which can distort performance by showing revenue in one period and related expenses in another. For example, a consulting firm completing a $50,000 project in December but receiving payment in January would show no December revenue under cash basis, misrepresenting that period's economic activity.

rubric.key: Clear explanation of accrual vs. cash basis

questions.question: Given the following data, calculate gross profit margin and current ratio: Revenue $500,000, COGS $300,000, Current Assets $180,000, Current Liabilities $90,000. Interpret what each ratio tells a potential investor.
questions.answer: Gross Profit Margin = (500,000 - 300,000) / 500,000 = 40%. Current Ratio = 180,000 / 90,000 = 2.0. The 40% gross margin means the company retains $0.40 from each dollar of revenue after direct costs, suggesting healthy pricing or cost control. The 2.0 current ratio means the company has twice the current assets needed to cover short-term obligations, indicating strong liquidity.

rubric.key: Accurate interpretation of gross profit margin

questions.question: A company reports net income of $200,000 but operating cash flow of only $50,000. Identify two possible explanations for this discrepancy.
questions.answer: 1) Large increase in accounts receivable: the company recognized revenue on credit but hasn't collected cash yet, inflating net income relative to cash flow. 2) Significant inventory build-up: the company purchased inventory (cash outflow) that hasn't yet been sold and expensed as COGS, reducing cash flow without affecting net income. Other valid explanations include prepaid expenses or timing of payables.

rubric.key: Demonstrates understanding of accrual vs. cash differences

major_assignments.title: Complete Accounting Cycle Project
major_assignments.description: Complete the full accounting cycle for a service business, starting from a list of January transactions through the preparation of all four financial statements and closing entries.
objectives: Record journal entries accurately using debit/credit rules
Post to T-accounts and prepare trial balances
Generate complete financial statements from adjusted data
deliverables: General journal with all entries including adjustments
Four financial statements (income statement, retained earnings, balance sheet, cash flows)

criteria: All accounts included with correct amounts
Income statement correctly prepared with proper format
Balance sheet in balance with correct classifications
Cash flow statement reconciles to balance sheet cash
Statement of retained earnings links income statement to balance sheet
Summary memo explaining key financial results
Balanced trial balances at each stage
Complete and correctly formatted financial statements

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of accounting concepts and calculations

homework_problems.description: Weekly problem sets practicing journal entries, adjustments, and analysis

major_assignments.description: Comprehensive accounting cycle and financial analysis projects

midterm_examination.description: Covers accounting cycle, adjusting entries, and income statement

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including all financial statements and ratio analysis

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory. Each unexcused absence after the second results in a 2% deduction from the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late homework loses 15% per day. Major assignments lose 10% per day with a maximum 3-day late window.
course_policies.collaboration: Homework may be discussed with peers but all submitted work must be independently completed. Show all calculations.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Using answer keys from prior semesters or solution manuals constitutes academic dishonesty and will result in a zero.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Scientific calculator required. Spreadsheet software (Excel or Google Sheets) recommended for financial statement projects.
